---
title: "Dataset Catalog"
format: html
---

# How to use this catalog

The default data source for Exercises 1 and 2 is the QCRC COVID-19 ICU workbook.
Use it unless another course dataset clearly fits your professional question
better.

If you switch datasets, keep the same report structure and add two sentences:

1. Why the alternate dataset fits your question better.
1. What limitation you accept by not using QCRC.

For download links, see [Course Resources](../references.qmd).

# Default dataset for Exercises 1 and 2

## QCRC COVID-19 ICU workbook

- File: `data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx`
- Main sheet: `Main_Dataset`
- Best use: Exercise 1, Exercise 2, formula syntax, logistic regression,
  descriptive tables, data-cleaning functions, and polished Quarto reporting.
- Unit of analysis for the main sheet: one ICU patient.
- Useful variables: age, sex, race, mortality endpoints, ICU length of stay,
  BMI, treatment indicator, intubation, CRRT, labs, and oxygenation measures.
- Common cleaning issue: some numeric-looking variables are stored as text, and
  missing values may appear as `"."`.

Other sheets in the workbook include `Oxygen_Delivery`, `SOFA`, `Long_Labs`,
`Long_Vitals`, `Static_Compliance`, `PAO2_FIO2`, `ICU_Meds`, `Intake_Form`, and
`Daily_Form`.

Use this import pattern for the main sheet:

```r
to_numeric <- function(x) {
  as.numeric(dplyr::na_if(as.character(x), "."))
}

qcrc <- readxl::read_xlsx(
  here::here("data", "QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx"),
  sheet = "Main_Dataset"
) |>
  janitor::clean_names() |>
  dplyr::mutate(
    bmi = to_numeric(bmi),
    crp = to_numeric(crp),
    d_dimer = to_numeric(d_dimer)
  )
```

# Course datasets

| Dataset | File or source | Used for | Good questions | Watch for |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| QCRC COVID-19 ICU workbook | `data/QCRC_FINAL_Deidentified.xlsx` | Exercises 1 and 2, formulas, model reporting, functional programming over workbook sheets | Patient-level factors associated with mortality, intubation, length of stay, or clinical markers | Numeric values stored as text, `"."` missing values, many sheets with different row levels |
| Measles vaccination and case data | `data/measles_final.Rds` | Iteration, functions, functional programming, repeated summaries, repeated models | Country or region trends in measles cases, vaccine coverage, or rates | Missing vaccine coverage, ecological interpretation, denominator choices |
| Simulated city cases | `data/city-cases.csv` | Aggregation, rates, time series summaries, disease-mapping-adjacent practice | City-month changes in cases by region, industry, exposure-like values, or temperature | Simulated data need clear framing; dates and rates require careful denominators |
| SIR observed data | `data/SIR-observed.csv` | ODE examples and optional ODE fitting | Comparing observed epidemic curves to simple simulated trajectories | Small and intentionally simplified; not a covariate-rich report dataset |
| Mosquito surveillance data | `data/mos-nyc.csv` | Optional likelihood, proportions, surveillance summaries, mapping-style examples | County-level mosquito pool positivity or infection risk | Aggregate pool data; assumptions about pool size matter |
| Birthweight data | `MASS::birthwt` package dataset | Bootstrapping examples | Risk ratios, resampling, confidence intervals | Not stored in `data/`; load from the `MASS` package |
| GIS data | `GIS/data/` | Disease mapping and GIS material | Spatial joins, mapping, and spatial public health examples | Best accessed from the full repository ZIP; not all GIS files are linked individually from the site |

# Which dataset should I choose?

Use QCRC when:

- you want the default Exercise 1 and 2 path,
- you need patient-level variables,
- you want to practice logistic regression and model reporting,
- you want a report that looks like a small manuscript.

Use measles when:

- your question is about repeated summaries over countries or regions,
- you want to practice loops, functions, or `lapply()`,
- you are comfortable with ecological interpretation.

Use city cases when:

- your question is about rates over time or places,
- you want a simulated but tidy public health example,
- you are practicing grouping, plotting, or mapping-adjacent logic.

Use SIR or mosquito data when:

- you are working through the ODE or optional likelihood material,
- you want a small focused modeling example rather than a full report dataset.

# Quick data checks

After loading any dataset, run a short check before analysis.

```r
dim(my_data)
names(my_data)
head(my_data)
summary(my_data)
```

For report work, also check missingness in the variables you plan to use.

```r
analysis_vars <- c("x30d_mortality", "age", "bmi", "intubated")

sapply(qcrc[analysis_vars], \(x) sum(is.na(x)))
```

# Reporting data limitations

Every report should include one plain limitation about the data. Good examples:

- "Some lab values were stored as text and required conversion before analysis."
- "This analysis estimates associations and does not establish causality."
- "The complete-case analysis may exclude patients with different clinical
  profiles."
- "The measles analysis is ecological; country-level associations may not
  describe individual risk."
